SENDER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

March 29, 1990

By Telecopy and by Federal Express

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974
    to Proposition 103 Applications for Rate Approval
    and Consolidated Hearing

Dear Ms. Ellison:

     This letter responds to your letter of February 14, 1990, in which you stated that the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") has received several inquiries regarding the application of the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 ("Act") to law firms and insurance companies participating in hearings relating to the implementation of Proposition 103. You requested information regarding:

(1) the nature and purpose of the current
    Proposition 103 hearings in San Bruno, California;

(2) what, if anything, distinguishes these hearings
    from being treated as "rate-making proceedings"
    pursuant to Section 82002 of the Act; and

(3) why law firms should not be treated as "lobbying
    firms."

     This letter is written on behalf of the Proposition 103 Joint Steering Committee 1/ (the "Committee") in response to your inquiries, and should supersede all previous correspondence addressed to you by the members of the Committee. In addition to responding specifically to your inquiries, this letter will provide further information which will assist you in determining the applicability of the Act to

MIFFLIN & CO.
BOSTON & NEW YORK

[Several lines of faint, illegible text at the top]


PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 2


(a) the Proposition 103 hearings in San Bruno, (b) rate
applications under Proposition 103, and (c) future hearings with
respect to those rate applications. Furthermore, the Committee
hereby requests advice pursuant to California Government Code
Section 83114(b) as to its members' and their clients' reporting
obligations under the Act.

1. STATEMENT OF FACTS

Proposition 103, which was adopted by the voters on
November 8, 1988, added a new Article 10 (Sections 1861.01-
1861.14) to Chapter 9 of Part 2 of Division 1 of the California
Insurance Code. In place of the then-existing "open
competition" system in California, Proposition 103 2/ required
insurers to reduce their rates immediately on policies issued or
renewed after November 8, 1988 to "at least 20 percent less"
than their rates in effect on November 8, 1987. Proposition 103
then established a system of insurance "rate approval," whereby
any insurer desiring to change a rate would have to file a rate
application with the Insurance Commissioner (the "Commissioner")
for prior approval. See Ins. Code §§ 1861.01 and
1861.05(b). Although this process of rate "approval" is new to
Chapter 9 of the Insurance Code, it is similar to "prior
approval" laws in many states. Proposition 103 did not
authorize the Commissioner to make or set rates.

A number of insurers challenged Proposition 103 and in
May, 1989, the California Supreme Court issued its decision in
CalFarm Ins. Co. v. Deukmejian, 48 Cal. 3d 805 (1989). In that
decision, the Supreme Court held that the rollback provision of
Proposition 103 was confiscatory on its face and could be
applied only if interpreted to permit insurers to earn a fair
and reasonable rate of return. The Court did not hold that the
proposition was otherwise unconstitutional, because the
Commissioner's decision would be subject to judicial review.
See Ins. Code § 1861.09. The Court also noted that the
completion of an adjudicatory record before the Commissioner
"will make possible a more informed analysis of any claim that
the rate set by Proposition 103 is confiscatory as to a
particular insurer and line of insurance." Id. at 825, n. 18.

Following this decision, insurers filed applications
for exemption from Proposition 103's rollback provision.
Beginning in August, 1989, the Department of Insurance began
noticing hearings on individual insurance company applications
to determine the fair rate of return for purposes of the

MIRIAM & TITTEN

[The rest of the page appears to be blank or contains only very faint, illegible text]

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 3


rollback, as required by CalFarm. These were noticed as
adjudicatory hearings.

     After two individual hearings had begun (Allstate and
Safeco), the Department decided to reformulate its hearing
schedule. It noticed hundreds of companies for individual
hearings and then bifurcated and consolidated those hearings so
that so-called "generic issues" would be decided first and then
company-specific issues would be addressed in individual
adjudicatory hearings. The generic phase of adjudication began
on October 30, 1989. Oral testimony concluded on March 1, 1990
and, subject to written submissions, the record will be closed
on April 9, 1990. Those hearings have been open, reported and
attended by counsel for insurers, consumers, the Attorney
General and the Department of Insurance. Witnesses have been
presented and cross-examined; evidence has been admitted and
excluded, and disputed issues have been ruled upon by
Administrative Law Judge William Fernandez. Judge Fernandez
was appointed, pursuant to an order dated October 30, 1989, to

     preside over all phases of the hearings, rule on
     all objections and motions, submit a Proposed
     Decision, including findings of fact and
     conclusions of law, to the Insurance
     Commissioner and . . . have such other powers as
     are ordinarily exercised by administrative law
     judges in the conduct of administrative
     hearings. (Emphasis added) 3/

The Notice of Bifurcation addressed to all participating parties
stated that "[t]he Consolidated Hearing . . . will be an
Adjudicatory Hearing . . . ." 4/  Moreover, Judge Fernandez and
the Insurance Commissioner have consistently taken the position
that the Consolidated Hearing is an adjudicatory hearing.
Finally, the Insurance Commissioner has suggested that these
hearings will be a one-time event.

     In applying for changes in insurance rates under
Proposition 103, insurers must submit to the Commissioner
certain information, including the number of policies written,
losses paid, number of claims paid, reserve information, certain
reinsurance information, and "such other information as the
Commissioner may require." Ins. Code § 1861.05(b). In
addition, the insurer must attempt to show in the application
"that the requested rate change is justified and meets the
requirements of [Proposition 103]." Id.

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 4


An application for a rate change is "deemed approved"
by the Commissioner sixty days after the Commissioner issues
public notice of the filed application. The application is not
deemed approved, however, if within the sixty-day approval
period: (1) a consumer requests a hearing within forty-five
days of public notice and the Commissioner either (a) grants
the hearing, or (b) determines not to grant the hearing and
issues written findings in support of that decision; (2) the
Commissioner decides to hold a hearing on his or her own motion;
or (3) the Commissioner holds a hearing "upon request" because
"the proposed rate adjustment exceeds 7% of the applicable rate
for personal lines or 15% for commercial lines or 15 for
commercial lines." Id.
§ 1861.05. As set forth in Section 1861.08 of the Insurance
Code, all hearings are adjudicatory in nature and are subject to
Chapter Five ("Administrative Adjudication") of the
Administrative Procedure Act ("APA").

2. DISCUSSION

A. The Proposition 103 Consolidated Hearing Is
Not a Rate-Making Proceeding Pursuant to
Section 82002 of the Act

1. The Reporting Requirements of the Act

The current California statutory provisions regulating
lobbying are found in the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the
"Act"). See Cal. Gov't. Code §§ 81000-91014 (1989). Adopted by
initiative in 1974, the Act imposes comprehensive regulations on
the activities of all parties who seek to influence state
legislative and administrative decision-making. The regulations
focus on the actions of lobbyists, lobbying firms, lobbyist
employers, and other persons spending $5,000 or more in a
calendar quarter to influence "legislative or administrative
action." See Gov't. Code § 86100 et seq.

Section 82002 of the Government Code defines
"administrative action" as

the proposal, drafting, development,
consideration, amendment, enactment or defeat by
any state agency of any rule, regulation or other
action in any rate-making proceeding or any
quasi-legislative proceeding, which shall include
any proceeding governed by Chapter 4.5 of
Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code
(beginning with Section 11371).

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 5


2.    Preliminary Approval Is Not Rate-
      Making or Quasi-Legislative Action

      a.    The Commissioner Has No Power Under
            Proposition 103 to Set Insurance Rates

      The FPPC has taken the position that applications for
preliminary approval of insurance rates constitute "rate-making
proceedings" under the Act. The Committee respectfully suggests
that this is not the case.

      As explained above, Proposition 103 requires insurers
to submit an application to the Commissioner if they seek a
change in their insurance rates. It must be emphasized,
however, that the Commissioner has no power to set or establish
rates in response to these applications. Indeed, if the
Commissioner decides not to act on an application (and no
consumer requests a hearing), the application is "deemed
approved." Ins. Code § 1861.05. If the Commissioner questions
an application, the most she can do is request a hearing, which,
as mentioned above, must be adjudicated in the first instance by
an administrative law judge, rather than the Commissioner.

      This activity clearly does not involve "rate-making."
Cf. Ins. Code § 12401 ("nothing in this article [regarding title
insurance rates] is intended to give the Commissioner power to
fix and determine a rate level by classification or
otherwise"). The Commissioner's limited power under
Proposition 103 to request a hearing stands in marked contrast
to the Commissioner's power to "set" or "make" other types of
insurance rates. See, e.g., Ins. Code §§ 11732 (approval of
classification of risks and premium rates for workers
compensation insurance) and 11820 (Commissioner's right to
"establish" rates to be charged by the State Compensation Fund).

      b.    Other States Have Determined That Rate
            Approval Is Not "Rate Making"

      The process of rate "approval" under Proposition 103
is similar to "prior approval" laws in approximately 20 other
states. These laws clearly indicate that such prior approval
does not constitute "rate-making." See, e.g., State Farm Mutual
Automobile Ins. Co. v. Barnes, 41 Colo. App. 380, 585 P.2d 929,
931 (under Colorado's property and casualty insurance statutes,
the Commissioner does not set rates, but approves or disapproves
rates submitted by an insurer); Nationwide Mut. Ins. Co. v.

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 6


Williams, 188 So. 2d 368, 369 (Fla. Dist. Ct. App. 1966)
(Commissioner's authority is limited to approving or
disapproving rates submitted by the insurer); Fire Insurance
Rating Bureau v. Rogan, 4 Wis. 2d 558, 91 N.W.2d 372, 376 (1958)
(Commissioner does not have the authority to establish rates);
State of North Carolina ex rel Commissioner of Insurance v.
North Carolina Fire Ins. Rating Bureau, 292 N.C. 471, 234 S.E.2d
720, 724 (1977) ("The Commissioner has no inherent power to fix
premium rates."); Monroe v. Insurance Services Office, 257 Ark
1018, 522 S.W.2d 428, 430 (1975) (Commissioner does not have the
power to fix a specified rate); Insurance Services Offices v.
State Board, 530 P.2d 1354, 1362 (Okla. 1975); Insurance
Services Offices v. Knutson, 283 N.W.2d 395, 397-398 (N.D. 1979).

       C.    The Consolidated Hearing Is More
             Closely Akin to Quasi-Judicial
             "Licensing" Hearings Than
             Quasi-Legislative Hearings

       In In re Evans, the FPPC noted the "dividing line"
between quasi-legislative action, which is treated as
administrative action under the Act, and quasi-judicial action,
which is not. "Generally speaking, a legislative action is the
formulation of a rule to be applied to all future acts, while an
adjudicatory act involves the actual application of such a rule
to a specific set of existing facts." In re Evans, 4 FPPC Ops.,
84, 89 (quoted in Strumsky v. San Diego County Employees'
Retirement Ass'n., 11 Cal. 3d 28, 35 n. 2 (1974)).

       The intent of the Act was not to require disclosure of
quasi-judicial administrative actions. Common Cause, one of the
drafters of the Act, never intended that the term
"administrative action" include quasi-judicial action. In a
California State Senate hearing shortly after adoption of the
Act, Larry Gotlieb, a representative of Common Cause, attempted
to respond to a concern by various labor groups that their local
officials would not be able to appear before various
administrative agencies in adjudicatory hearings where laws or
rules are applied, without having to register as a lobbyist.
Mr. Gotlieb stated:

       It has been the position of Common Cause that
       appearing in a hearing in which an employee's
       right to compensation, his right to a license or
       permit or any similar hearing in which a law or
       rule is being applied in a discretionary or

BERTRAM & TITTERS

[illegible]

[illegible]
i
ii

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 7


                    ministerial fashion in a particular case is not
                    an act of lobbying. 5/

                    In an April 22, 1974 memorandum, Common Cause
explained its interpretation of the Act's definition of
"administrative action":

                    Administrative action is restricted to
                    legislative-type action by state administrative
                    agencies -- to adoption of regulations, rule
                    making, rate making.  It does not apply to the
                    application of law, rule, or discretion to
                    particular cases but only to quasi-legislative
                    action to administrative law-making.  For
                    example, a hearing on an employee's right to
                    compensation or a person's right to a license or
                    permit under a statute or regulation would not
                    be an "administrative action" under the Act. 6/

                    Common Cause's interpretation of the action that is
not covered under the Act's definition of administrative action,
e.g., quasi-judicial action, closely tracks the APA definition
of an adjudicatory hearing.  See Gov't Code § 11500(f)
("[adjudicatory hearing" means a state agency hearing which
involves the personal or property rights of an individual, the
granting or revocation of an individual's license, or the
resolution of an issue pertaining to an individual").

                    Because review of individual insurance applications
under Proposition 103, individual hearings thereon, and the
Consolidated Hearing are clearly the type of quasi-judicial or
adjudicatory action to which the drafters of the Act did not
intend the Act apply, these actions cannot be considered
"rate-making" proceedings or quasi-legislative action under the
Act.

                    The Commissioner's review of applications submitted
by particular companies pursuant to Proposition 103 is
necessarily quasi-judicial or fact-specific, for two reasons.
First, because each insurer has submitted its own application
for approval, and second, because the Commissioner's "approval"
of an application in essence gives an insurance company a
"license or entitlement" to issue insurance. As the FPPC noted
in Evans, 4 FPPC Ops. 84, 88-89 (1978):

WITHDRAWN AUCTION

[The rest of the page appears to be blank or too faint to read with certainty]

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 8


In permit or licensing proceedings, the
rights, duties, or obligations of a single
person or entity is [sic] at stake and no
decision of general application is made. Permit
and licensing decisions have been considered to
be quasi-judicial ones because they most often
involve application of a general standard to a
particular set of facts presented by an
individual applicant. In this respect, such
proceedings most resemble the decision-making
processes of courts rather than legislative
bodies. [Citations omitted.]

Applications for insurance rate approval clearly fall
within the definition of quasi-judicial action discussed by the
FPPC in In re Evans and contemplated by the drafters of the
Act. As a decision is only being applied to individual
insurers, and as it is binding only on the person seeking this
"entitlement," it is quasi-judicial.

The fact that the Consolidated Hearing will apply to
more than one company does not change its nature under the Act.
Although the Hearing addresses generic issues regarding rate
filings it will not result in actual rate-setting for the
companies. The hearing was consolidated not to set rates for
all companies, but rather to attempt to determine generic facts
and investigate the facts in one proceeding for the sake of
convenience and speed. Thus, even if this common fact-finding
may ultimately provide a common framework for addressing
individual company rate applications, it is not legislative
action that determines rates for the entire industry.

3. Background and History of the Term
"Rate-Making Proceeding"

The legislative history and context of Section 82002
of the Act also confirm that the term "rate-making proceedings"
was never intended to apply to insurance rate applications or
hearings in connection with applications which constitute
quasi-judicial administrative action. Instead, the term was
intended to apply to quasi-legislative rate-making, which
existed in 1974, when the Act was passed, and with which the
voters were presumably familiar.

The definition of administrative action was drafted to
include "rate-making proceeding" to make clear that standard

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 9


Public Utility Commission ("PUC") rate-making proceedings were
quasi-legislative administrative action that should be included
in the Act. Rate-making proceedings before the PUC are quasi-
legislative proceedings. See Pacific Tel. & Tel.
Co. v. Public Util. Comm'n, 62 Cal. 2d 634 (1965) (the fixing or
reduction of a rate for a public utility in the first instance
is prospective in application and legislative in character).
Indeed, the California Supreme Court has characterized the PUC's
rate-making proceedings involving "the fixing of a rate" as
quasi-legislative, distinguishing them from quasi-judicial
proceedings. See Consumers Lobby Against Monopolies v. Public
Util. Comm'n, 25 Cal. 3d 891, 909 (1979). The California
Constitution and the legislature expressly authorized the PUC to
"fix rates" for transportation companies and public utilities
within its jurisdiction. Cal. Const., art. 12, §§ 4, 6; Cal.
Pub. Util. Code § 728.

In contrast, the Insurance Commissioner does not have
the power to "make" rates under Proposition 103. This contrasts
with her quasi-legislative "rate-making" power as to certain
other types of insurance, such as workers' compensation. See
State Compensation Ins. Fund v. F. Britton McConnell, 46 Cal. 2d
330 (1956) (insurance commissioner's premium discount plan and
retrospective rating plan for workers' compensation insurance
premium rating were "regulations" fixing and establishing rates).

Furthermore, the drafters of the Act specifically
included PUC rate-making proceedings, because those are not
governed by the rulemaking provisions of the California
Administrative Procedure Act. See Gov't. Code § 11351(a)
(exempting PUC proceedings from almost all provisions of APA).
The Insurance Commissioner, however, with few exceptions, is
subject to APA's rulemaking provisions if the Commissioner
issues a rule or regulation. See, e.g., Ins. Code § 12401.5(b)
(regulation prescribing any statistical plan for title insurance
rates must meet APA requirements). Therefore, there is no need
for the additional protection of the Act's reporting
requirements.

The FPPC's historical interpretation of the term
"rate-making proceedings" also supports the interpretation that
the term was specifically intended to apply to the PUC form of
quasi-legislative rate-making proceeding and should not be
extended to the current Proposition 103 hearings. The FPPC has
apparently applied the concept of "rate-making proceeding" only
to standard Public Utilities Commission proceedings for setting

КНИГАМ И ПУБЛЕЙ

[illegible text at bottom of page]

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 10


of rates in the utilities or transportation industries. The
Letter to Ed Perez, September 13, 1977, No. A-77-143 ("Perez
Advice Letter"), upon which the FPPC's more recent letters
regarding actions under Proposition 103 are based, states "the
term 'rate-making proceeding' means any proceeding involving the
establishment of rates." Perez Advice Letter p. 1 (emphasis
added). Because the Commissioner has no power to "establish"
rates, hearings relating to Proposition 103 applications for
rate approval cannot be considered "rate-making procedures."
Indeed, the FPPC's only regulations dealing with rate-making
proceedings are targeted at these PUC rate-making proceedings,
and not at the current Proposition 103 hearings. See
2 California Code of Regulations ("C.C.R.") §§ 18615 and 18616.

The definition of rate-making under California case
law also supports an interpretation that the term "rate-making
proceeding" in Section 82002 of the Act refers only to
rate-making that is quasi-legislative in nature, rather than to
quasi-judicial proceedings like the Consolidated Hearing. In
City Council v. Superior Court, 179 Cal. App. 2d 389, 393
(1960), the court of appeal, in reviewing a refusal to grant
higher garbage collection taxes, stated that rate-making, or
"[t]he fixing or refixing of rates for a public service is
legislative, or at least quasi-legislative." In contrast,
proceedings in connection with "company specific" rate approval
applications are clearly adjudicatory by statutory
prescription. Ins. Code § 1861.08. See CalFarm, 48 Cal.3d at
824.

California cases and statutes create an important
procedural distinction between quasi-judicial and
quasi-legislative proceedings. In adjudicatory (or
quasi-judicial) proceedings like the San Bruno hearings, parties
are afforded certain rights typically attendant to a court
proceeding, such as the opportunity to cross-examine witnesses.
These rights are not granted in a quasi-legislative proceeding.
Moreover, the scope of judicial review of a quasi-judicial
administrative proceeding is different from the scope of
judicial review for a rulemaking proceeding 7/. Judicial review
for adjudicatory actions is governed by administrative mandamus,
under a "substantial evidence" standard of review, while
judicial review of quasi-legislative actions is governed by
ordinary mandamus, to determine whether the agency acted
arbitrarily or capriciously, with no evidentiary support, or did
not comply with prescribed procedures. See Cal. City. Proc. Code
Section 1094.5(b) (standard for administrative mandamus); Cal.

PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 11


Gov't. Code § 11350 (standard for ordinary mandamus); Rivera v.
Division of Industrial Welfare, 265 Cal. App. 2d 586 (1968)
(judicial review of regulations).

To summarize, the legislative history and subsequent
application by the FPPC of the term "rate-making proceedings"
make clear that the term was meant to apply to a
quasi-legislative form of proceeding, and probably only to
"rate-making proceedings" before the PUC. The term was not
meant to apply to hearings like the San Bruno proceedings.

4.  Hearings on Individual Applications
    Generally

Company-specific hearings, which will follow the
pending Consolidated Hearing, are clearly adjudicatory hearings,
and therefore are not "rate-making proceedings" under the Act.
As discussed above, if a rate application is "deemed approved,"
there will be no hearing on that application. Proposition 103
contemplates that a hearing will only take place if a consumer
or the Commissioner requests a hearing on the rate application.
Such a hearing will be governed by the portion of the APA
dealing with adjudicatory hearings, and the Commissioner must
appoint an ALJ to conduct the hearing. In addition, these
hearings contemplate cross-examination of witnesses and limited
discovery. See Ins. Code § 1861.08; Gov't. Code § 11507.6. The
issue in such a hearing would be whether the rate proposed in
the rate application meets the "excessive/inadequate" standard
of Proposition 103 and the "fair and reasonable" standard
established in CalFarm.

Thus, this is clearly "quasi-judicial" action, as
defined by the FPPC (see 2 C.C.R. §§ 18202(a)(1) ("a proceeding
to determine the rights or duties of a person under existing
laws, regulations or policies") and 18202(a)(7) ("a proceeding
involving the issuance of a legal opinion")), and is not the
type of situation "where disclosure is intended or justified."
In re Evans, 4 FPPC Ops. at 92.

5.  Future Rule and Regulation Making
    Hearings

The Commissioner has suggested that following the
Consolidated Hearing, the Commissioner may hold hearings to
establish rules and regulations for rate rollbacks. Such
hearings will also determine appropriate ranges for general

MITRAM & TITTER

[illegible]


PETTIT & MARTIN

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
March 29, 1990
Page 12


rates of return. The Commissioner will conduct such hearings in
accordance with California Government Code Sections 11340-11446,
which provide the Office of Administrative Law ("OAL") with
jurisdiction over any exercise of quasi-legislative power, with
certain exceptions. 8/

Every state agency which adopts rules and regulations
must submit its newly adopted regulations to the OAL for its
approval. The office must either approve or disapprove a
regulation within 30 days of its delivery to the OAL, and
failure to act by the office constitutes automatic approval.
Cal. Gov't Code § 11349.3. In addition, Government Code
Section 11346.4 requires a state agency to give the OAL 45 days
notice prior to the hearing on adoption of a new rule or
regulation. Thus, no rules or regulations may be issued as a
result of the current Proposition 103 hearings. The
promulgation of any new rules or regulations which may be issued
by the Commissioner in the future as a result of Proposition 103
must comply with the OAL's rule and regulation making procedures.

This rule making activity is not the same as the
current Consolidated Hearing or any future individual
Application Hearings. As already explained, the Consolidated
Hearing is an administrative hearing before an ALJ who is a
retired, experienced superior court judge, which is akin to a
nonjury trial in a civil court and is governed by the APA,
Government Code Sections 11340-11528. The APA hearing is an
adjudicatory hearing which provides for formal discovery
procedures, abides by the rules of evidence, and follows a
simplified method of procedure modeled after the rules of civil
procedure. Furthermore, litigants in APA hearings are entitled
to the minimum standards of notice and a fair hearing before
being deprived of liberty or property interests. California
Administrative Hearing Practice § 1.3, CEB (1984).

Thus, it is clear that the Consolidated Hearing is an

Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974 to Proposition 103 Applications for Rate Approval and Consolidated Hearing Dear Ms. Ellison: This letter responds to your letter of February 14, 1990, in which you stated that the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") has received several inquiries regarding the application of the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 ("Act") to law firms and insurance companies participating in hearings relating to the implementation of Proposition 103. You requested information regarding: (1) the nature and purpose of the current Proposition 103 hearings in San Bruno, California; (2) what, if anything, distinguishes these hearings from being treated as "rate-making proceedings" pursuant to Section 82002 of the Act; and (3) why law firms should not be treated as "lobbying firms."
